ud3.6:1.2: on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Rājagaha at the Bamboo Forest, the Squirrels’ refuge.
ud3.6:1.3: Now at on that occasion Ven. Pilindavaccha went around addressing the monks as if they were outcastes.
ud3.6:1.4:
So a large number of monks went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, bowed down to him and sat to one side.
As they were sitting there they said to him,
ud3.6:1.5: “Lord, Ven. Pilindavaccha goes around addressing the monks as if they were outcastes.”
ud3.6:2.1: Then the Blessed One told a certain monk,
ud3.6:2.2:
“Come, monk.
In my name, call Pilindavaccha, saying,
ud3.6:2.4:
Responding,
“As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, the monk went to Ven. Pilindavaccha and on arrival said to him,
ud3.6:3.1:
Responding,
“As you say, my friend,” to the monk, Ven. Pilindavaccha went to the Blessed One and,
on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side.
As he was sitting there, the Blessed One said to him,
ud3.6:3.2: “Is it true, Pilindavaccha, that you go around addressing the monks as if they were outcastes?”
ud3.6:4.1: Then the Blessed One, having directed attention to Ven. Pilindavaccha’s previous lives, said to the monks,
ud3.6:4.2: “Don’t take offense at the monk Vaccha.
ud3.6:4.3: It’s not out of inner hatred that he goes around addressing the monks as if they were outcastes.
ud3.6:4.4: For 500 consecutive lifetimes the monk Vaccha has been born in brahman families.
ud3.6:4.5: For a long time he has been accustomed to addressing people as outcastes.
ud3.6:4.6: That’s why he goes around addressing the monks as if they were outcastes.”
ud3.6:5.1: Then, on realizing the significance of that, the Blessed One on that occasion exclaimed:
ud3.6:6.1: In whom there’s no deceit or conceit,
ud3.6:6.2: his greed ended, unpossessive, free from longing,
ud3.6:6.3: his anger dispelled, his mind unbound:
ud3.6:6.4:
He’s a contemplative.
He’s a brahman :
a monk.
